message:
Enhance FrankenTUI beads with testing infrastructure and critical features

Added 12 new beads for comprehensive coverage:

TESTING INFRASTRUCTURE:
- bd-3e8: Structured logging with tracing crate
- bd-2vr: Comprehensive E2E test suite (scripts/e2e/)
- bd-xn2: CI/CD pipeline configuration
- bd-19x: Performance benchmark suite with budgets
- bd-3hy: Per-module unit test coverage requirements
- bd-397: Adversarial security tests (escape injection)

CRITICAL MISSING FEATURES:
- bd-fbp: Inline mode overlay redraw baseline (P0)
- bd-2zd: Scroll-region optimization for inline mode
- bd-29v: Viewport/LogViewer widget (agent harness essential)
- bd-1f8: Color downgrade (TrueColor → 256 → 16 → mono)
- bd-1zv: Event coalescing + RAII terminal guard
- bd-35p: Progress and Spinner widgets

Total beads: 73 (was 61)
Dependencies: 81 edges
No cycles detected
Actionable: 15 ready to start

All beads verified against Master TODO Inventory from plan v6.4.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-10x","title":"Implement Style struct with cascading/merging","description":"PHASE 4 - STYLING CORE

## Background
Style struct is the unified styling type that combines colors, attributes, and modifiers into a single composable unit. Must support cascading/merging like CSS inheritance.

## Technical Requirements
- Style struct containing:
  - foreground: Option<PackedRgba>
  - background: Option<PackedRgba>
  - attrs: Option<CellAttrs>
  - underline_color: Option<PackedRgba>
- Cascading merge: child.merge(parent) fills in None fields from parent
- Override merge: parent.patch(child) replaces with child's Some values
- Builder pattern for ergonomic construction
- impl Default with all None (transparent/inheriting)

## Design Rationale
Option-wrapped fields enable CSS-like inheritance where unset properties inherit from parent context. This matches user mental model from web development.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Style struct with Option-wrapped components
- [ ] merge() for cascading (child wins on conflict)
- [ ] patch() for override (explicit values win)
- [ ] Builder pattern: Style::new().fg(color).bold()
- [ ] #[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
- [ ] Zero-cost when all fields are None
- [ ] Property tests for merge associativity

## Performance Budget
- Style merge: < 5ns (branchless bitfield ops)
- sizeof(Style) ≤ 24 bytes

 {"id":"bd-16k","title":"Unicode width corpus testing with WTF-8","description":"TESTING - UNICODE WIDTH CORPUS

## Background
Unicode width calculation is notoriously complex with edge cases around emoji, CJK, combining characters, and ZWJ sequences. A comprehensive test corpus is essential for correctness.

## Technical Requirements
- Test corpus categories:
  - Basic ASCII (width 1)
  - CJK Unified Ideographs (width 2)
  - Fullwidth ASCII variants (width 2)
  - Halfwidth Katakana (width 1)
  - Emoji with modifiers (width varies)
  - ZWJ sequences (flag emoji, family emoji)
  - Combining characters (width 0)
  - Control characters (width 0)
  - Ambiguous width characters (context-dependent)
- WTF-8 handling for unpaired surrogates
- Terminal-specific width (vs Unicode Standard)
- Test against unicode-width crate results

## Known Edge Cases
- 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 (family emoji ZWJ) - varies by terminal
- 🏳️‍🌈 (flag ZWJ) - often renders as 2 cells
- Regional indicators (flag pairs)
- Variation selectors (VS15/VS16)

## Design Rationale
Correctness in width calculation directly impacts rendering quality. The corpus captures real-world edge cases that simple tests miss.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Corpus with 1000+ test cases
- [ ] Categorized by width type
- [ ] Known terminal-specific behaviors documented
- [ ] Property tests for grapheme iteration
- [ ] Fuzz testing for malformed input
- [ ] Benchmark for width calculation performance

+{"id":"bd-19x","title":"Create performance benchmark suite","description":"TESTING - PERFORMANCE BENCHMARKS

## Background
FrankenTUI has strict performance budgets from the plan. Benchmarks must verify these budgets are met and prevent regressions.

## Performance Budgets (from plan)

### Core Operations
| Operation | Target | Panic Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|
| Cell comparison | < 1ns | < 5ns |
| Cell bits_eq SIMD | < 0.5ns | < 2ns |
| Row comparison (80 cols) | < 100ns | < 500ns |
| Buffer diff (80×24) | < 10µs | < 50µs |

### Present Operations
| Scenario | p50 Target | p99 Target |
|----------|------------|------------|
| 80×24, 5% changed | < 1.0ms | < 3.0ms |
| 80×24, 50% changed | < 2.0ms | < 5.0ms |
| 200×50, 5% changed | < 2.0ms | < 5.0ms |
| 200×50, full redraw | < 10ms | < 25ms |

### Layout Operations
| Operation | Target | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Flex solve (10 items) | < 50µs | |
| Flex solve (50 items) | < 200µs | |
| Grid solve (10×10) | < 500µs | |

### Widget Rendering
| Widget | Target (per instance) |
|--------|----------------------|
| Block | < 50µs |
| Paragraph (100 chars) | < 100µs |
| Table (100 rows visible) | < 500µs |

## Technical Requirements

### Benchmark Infrastructure
- Use criterion crate
- benches/ directory structure
- Statistical analysis with confidence intervals
- Comparison against baseline

### Benchmark Organization
benches/
├── cell_bench.rs      # Cell operations
├── buffer_bench.rs    # Buffer operations  
├── diff_bench.rs      # Diff algorithms
├── present_bench.rs   # Presenter operations
├── layout_bench.rs    # Layout solving
├── widget_bench.rs    # Widget rendering
└── e2e_bench.rs       # Full pipeline

### Each Benchmark Must
1. Warm up properly
2. Use black_box to prevent optimization
3. Test multiple input sizes
4. Report memory allocation counts
5. Compare against budget threshold

### Benchmark Example
use criterion::{black_box, criterion_group, criterion_main, Criterion};

fn cell_comparison(c: &mut Criterion) {
    let cell1 = Cell::default();
    let cell2 = Cell::char('a');
    
    c.bench_function("cell_bits_eq_same", |b| {
        b.iter(|| black_box(&cell1).bits_eq(black_box(&cell1)))
    });
    
    c.bench_function("cell_bits_eq_diff", |b| {
        b.iter(|| black_box(&cell1).bits_eq(black_box(&cell2)))
    });
}

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cell comparison benchmarks
- [ ] Buffer operation benchmarks
- [ ] Diff algorithm benchmarks
- [ ] Present pipeline benchmarks
- [ ] Layout solver benchmarks
- [ ] Widget render benchmarks
- [ ] Budget verification in CI
- [ ] Baseline comparison reports
- [ ] Memory allocation tracking

+{"id":"bd-1f8","title":"Implement color downgrade (TrueColor → 256 → 16 → mono)","description":"PHASE 4 - COLOR DOWNGRADE SYSTEM

## Background
Not all terminals support TrueColor. The color system must gracefully downgrade colors to match terminal capabilities while maintaining visual coherence.

## Technical Requirements

### Color Profiles (from capabilities)
pub enum ColorProfile {
    Mono,      // No color (dumb terminal)
    Ansi16,    // 16 colors (basic)
    Ansi256,   // 256 colors (xterm)
    TrueColor, // 24-bit RGB
}

### Downgrade Chain
TrueColor → 256 → 16 → Mono

### Algorithms

#### RGB to 256-color
fn rgb_to_256(r: u8, g: u8, b: u8) -> u8 {
    // Grayscale check first
    if r == g && g == b {
        if r < 8 { return 16; }
        if r > 248 { return 231; }
        return 232 + ((r - 8) / 10).min(23);
    }
    // 6×6×6 color cube
    let r6 = (r as u16 * 6 / 256) as u8;
    let g6 = (g as u16 * 6 / 256) as u8;
    let b6 = (b as u16 * 6 / 256) as u8;
    16 + 36 * r6 + 6 * g6 + b6
}

#### 256-color to 16-color
- Map cube colors to nearest ANSI color
- Preserve bright/normal distinction
- Use perceptual color distance (not Euclidean RGB)

#### 16-color to Mono
- Convert to brightness value
- Above threshold = white, below = black
- Optionally use bold for emphasis

### Caching
- Cache downgraded colors (LRU or hash)
- Profile is detected once at startup
- Colors resolved at render time

### Theme Integration
Themes should define colors at highest fidelity.
Presenter applies downgrade based on detected profile.

## Tests Required
- [ ] TrueColor passthrough works
- [ ] 256-color conversion accurate
- [ ] 16-color conversion visually reasonable
- [ ] Mono fallback works
- [ ] Grayscale optimization
- [ ] Cache hit rate acceptable
- [ ] Profile detection accurate

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All profiles supported
- [ ] Downgrade preserves intent
- [ ] No performance regression
- [ ] Theme colors degrade gracefully
- [ ] Works in tmux/screen

 {"id":"bd-1qb","title":"Implement Image widget with protocol detection","description":"PHASE 7 - EXTRAS: IMAGE WIDGET

## Background
Image widget displays images in terminal using Kitty Graphics Protocol, iTerm2 inline images, or Sixel fallback. Requires protocol detection and graceful degradation.

## Technical Requirements
- Protocol detection:
  - Kitty Graphics Protocol (XTGETTCAP query)
  - iTerm2 inline images (TERM_PROGRAM check)
  - Sixel (DA2 response parsing)
  - Fallback: ASCII/Braille representation
- Image loading via image crate
- Resize/fit modes: Contain, Cover, Stretch, None
- Aspect ratio preservation
- Partial update for animated GIFs
- Memory management for large images

## Design Rationale
Image support is a premium feature gated behind 'image' flag. Protocol detection happens once at startup and caches result. Fallback ensures graceful degradation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Protocol detection and caching
- [ ] Kitty Graphics Protocol implementation
- [ ] iTerm2 inline image implementation
- [ ] Sixel fallback (optional sub-feature)
- [ ] ASCII fallback when no protocol available
- [ ] Resize mode configuration
- [ ] Aspect ratio handling
- [ ] Feature gate: image

## Performance Budget
- Protocol detection: < 50ms (one-time)
- Image encode/transmit: proportional to size

+{"id":"bd-1zv","title":"Implement event coalescing and RAII terminal guard","description":"PHASE 3 - EVENT COALESCING + TERMINAL GUARD

## Background
Event storms (mouse moves, resize spam) can overwhelm the runtime. RAII guards ensure terminal cleanup even on panic.

## Technical Requirements

### Event Coalescing
pub struct EventCoalescer {
    pending_mouse_move: Option<MouseEvent>,
    pending_resize: Option<(u16, u16)>,
    coalesce_window_ms: u64,
}

impl EventCoalescer {
    /// Add event, returns coalesced event if ready
    pub fn push(&mut self, event: Event) -> Option<Event> {
        match event {
            Event::Mouse(MouseEvent::Move { .. }) => {
                // Replace pending, return only on next non-move
                self.pending_mouse_move = Some(event);
                None
            }
            Event::Resize(w, h) => {
                // Keep latest resize
                self.pending_resize = Some((w, h));
                None
            }
            _ => {
                // Flush pending events, then return this one
                self.flush_pending();
                Some(event)
            }
        }
    }
    
    pub fn flush_pending(&mut self) -> Vec<Event> { ... }
}

### RAII Terminal Guard

pub struct TerminalGuard {
    raw_mode: bool,
    alt_screen: bool,
    mouse_capture: bool,
    bracketed_paste: bool,
    cursor_hidden: bool,
}

impl TerminalGuard {
    pub fn new() -> io::Result<Self> {
        // Install panic hook
        let prev_hook = std::panic::take_hook();
        std::panic::set_hook(Box::new(move |info| {
            // Restore terminal state
            let _ = disable_raw_mode();
            let _ = execute\!(stdout(), LeaveAlternateScreen, Show);
            prev_hook(info);
        }));
        
        Ok(Self { ... })
    }
    
    pub fn enable_raw_mode(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> { ... }
    pub fn enter_alt_screen(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> { ... }
    pub fn enable_mouse_capture(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> { ... }
    pub fn enable_bracketed_paste(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> { ... }
    pub fn hide_cursor(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> { ... }
}

impl Drop for TerminalGuard {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // Restore all state in reverse order
        if self.cursor_hidden {
            let _ = execute\!(stdout(), Show);
        }
        if self.bracketed_paste {
            let _ = execute\!(stdout(), DisableBracketedPaste);
        }
        if self.mouse_capture {
            let _ = execute\!(stdout(), DisableMouseCapture);
        }
        if self.alt_screen {
            let _ = execute\!(stdout(), LeaveAlternateScreen);
        }
        if self.raw_mode {
            let _ = disable_raw_mode();
        }
    }
}

## Tests Required
- [ ] Mouse move coalescing works
- [ ] Resize coalescing works
- [ ] Key events pass through
- [ ] Guard enables/disables correctly
- [ ] Drop restores all state
- [ ] Panic hook restores terminal
- [ ] PTY test: panic cleanup

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No event storms reach runtime
- [ ] Terminal always restored on exit
- [ ] Panic leaves terminal usable
- [ ] Nested guards work correctly
- [ ] Mouse flood doesn't cause lag

+{"id":"bd-29v","title":"Implement Viewport/LogViewer widget (agent harness essential)","description":"PHASE 5 - VIEWPORT/LOG VIEWER WIDGET (ESSENTIAL)

## Background
The Viewport/LogViewer is THE ESSENTIAL widget for agent harness UIs. It displays streaming logs with scrollback while maintaining UI chrome. This is the primary use case for FrankenTUI.

## Technical Requirements

### Core Functionality
- Display scrolling text content
- Append new lines efficiently (O(1))
- Scroll to bottom on new content (auto-scroll)
- Manual scrolling (up/down/page/home/end)
- Scroll position indicator
- Max line count with circular buffer

### API Design

pub struct LogViewer {
    lines: VecDeque<Text>,
    max_lines: usize,
    scroll_offset: usize,
    auto_scroll: bool,
    style: Style,
}

impl LogViewer {
    pub fn new(max_lines: usize) -> Self;
    
    /// Append a line (triggers auto-scroll if enabled)
    pub fn push(&mut self, line: impl Into<Text>);
    
    /// Append multiple lines efficiently
    pub fn push_many(&mut self, lines: impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Into<Text>>);
    
    /// Scroll commands
    pub fn scroll_up(&mut self, lines: usize);
    pub fn scroll_down(&mut self, lines: usize);
    pub fn scroll_to_top(&mut self);
    pub fn scroll_to_bottom(&mut self);
    pub fn page_up(&mut self);
    pub fn page_down(&mut self);
    
    /// Toggle auto-scroll
    pub fn set_auto_scroll(&mut self, enabled: bool);
    
    /// Check if scrolled to bottom
    pub fn is_at_bottom(&self) -> bool;
}

impl StatefulWidget for LogViewer {
    type State = LogViewerState;
    fn render(&self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer, state: &mut Self::State);
}

### Rendering Strategy
1. Calculate visible lines based on area height
2. Determine start index from scroll_offset
3. Render visible lines with wrapping/truncation
4. Update scroll indicator
5. Handle partially visible lines at edges

### Line Wrapping
- Support: NoWrap, CharWrap, WordWrap modes
- Handle wide characters correctly
- Preserve styling across wrapped lines

### Performance Requirements
- Push: O(1) amortized
- Render: O(visible_lines)
- Memory: bounded by max_lines

### Mouse Support (optional)
- Scroll wheel for scrolling
- Click to select line (for copy)
- Drag to select text

## Tests Required
- [ ] Push appends correctly
- [ ] Circular buffer eviction works
- [ ] Auto-scroll behavior
- [ ] Manual scroll operations
- [ ] Wrapping modes work
- [ ] Wide character handling
- [ ] Visible range calculation
- [ ] Performance: 10k lines, 60 FPS scroll

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Streaming logs work smoothly
- [ ] Scroll position indicator accurate
- [ ] Auto-scroll toggles correctly
- [ ] Memory bounded by max_lines
- [ ] No flicker during append
- [ ] Works with inline mode

 {"id":"bd-2a5","title":"API documentation and examples suite","description":"PHASE 7 - POLISH: DOCUMENTATION

## Background
Comprehensive documentation is essential for library adoption. Must include API docs, guides, and runnable examples covering all major use cases.

## Technical Requirements

### API Documentation
- All public types documented with /// comments
- Module-level documentation with usage examples
- Cross-linking between related types
- Platform-specific behavior documented
- Feature flag documentation
- Safety documentation for any unsafe code

### Examples Suite
- examples/hello.rs - Minimal 'Hello World'
- examples/counter.rs - Basic state management
- examples/input.rs - Text input handling
- examples/layout.rs - Flex/Grid layouts
- examples/styling.rs - Themes and styles
- examples/table.rs - Data tables
- examples/async.rs - Async runtime integration
- examples/inline.rs - Inline mode demo

### Guide Documentation
- Getting Started guide
- Architecture overview
- Migration guide from Ratatui
- Performance tuning guide
- Testing guide

## Design Rationale
Documentation is a feature. Examples serve as both learning resources and regression tests. Keeping docs close to code via rustdoc ensures accuracy.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 100% public API documented
- [ ] All examples compile and run
- [ ] Examples cover each major widget
- [ ] Guide for each major topic

 {"id":"bd-2m5","title":"SIMD-accelerated cell comparison and buffer operations","description":"PERFORMANCE - SIMD OPTIMIZATION

## Background
Cell comparison dominates diff performance. The 16-byte Cell design enables 4 cells per 64-byte cache line, optimized for SIMD comparison. This task implements SIMD acceleration.

## Technical Requirements
- SIMD cell equality comparison:
  - Compare 4 cells (64 bytes) at once with AVX2
  - Fallback to 2 cells (32 bytes) with SSE2
  - Scalar fallback for non-x86
- Buffer row comparison:
  - Find first differing cell in row
  - Find changed row spans for minimal diff
- Memory alignment:
  - Buffer rows aligned to 64 bytes
  - Cell array uses aligned allocation

## Implementation Strategy
1. Use std::simd (portable_simd) when stabilized
2. Until then: safe abstraction over platform intrinsics
3. Feature gate: 'simd' (enabled by default on x86_64)
4. Graceful fallback with #[cfg] guards

## Design Rationale
The plan explicitly calls out SIMD as the optimization path for cell comparison. 16-byte Cell alignment is specifically chosen to enable 4-way comparison.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SIMD cell comparison implementation
- [ ] AVX2 path for x86_64
- [ ] SSE2 fallback
- [ ] Scalar fallback for other platforms
- [ ] Benchmark showing speedup
- [ ] No unsafe without exhaustive safety comments
- [ ] Runtime feature detection where appropriate

## Performance Budget
- Cell comparison: < 1ns (target from plan)
- Row comparison: < 10ns per cell
- Buffer diff: sublinear with SIMD

+{"id":"bd-2zd","title":"Implement scroll-region optimization for inline mode","description":"PHASE 2.5 - SCROLL REGION OPTIMIZATION

## Background
Scroll regions (DECSTBM) can optimize inline mode by pinning the UI region and letting logs scroll naturally. This is an OPTIMIZATION, not the baseline.

## Technical Requirements

### DECSTBM Usage
- Set scroll region to exclude UI: ESC[1;{log_bottom}r
- Logs scroll within region, UI stays pinned
- Must restore scroll region on exit

### Capability Gating
Only use when:
- caps.scroll_region == true
- NOT in tmux/screen/zellij (mux passthrough issues)
- Terminal known to support correctly

### Implementation

pub struct ScrollRegionPresenter {
    ui_height: u16,
    scroll_region_active: bool,
}

impl ScrollRegionPresenter {
    pub fn setup_scroll_region(&mut self, writer: &mut TerminalWriter) {
        if !self.can_use_scroll_region() {
            return;  // Fall back to overlay
        }
        
        let (_, height) = writer.size();
        let log_bottom = height - self.ui_height;
        
        // Set scroll region (excludes UI at bottom)
        write!(writer, "\x1b[1;{}r", log_bottom)?;
        self.scroll_region_active = true;
    }
    
    pub fn restore_scroll_region(&mut self, writer: &mut TerminalWriter) {
        if self.scroll_region_active {
            writer.write_all(b"\x1b[r")?;  // Reset to full screen
            self.scroll_region_active = false;
        }
    }
    
    fn can_use_scroll_region(&self) -> bool {
        let caps = TerminalCapabilities::detect();
        caps.scroll_region && 
        !caps.in_tmux &&
        !caps.in_screen &&
        !caps.in_zellij
    }
}

### Mux Detection
- TMUX env var presence
- TERM containing "screen"
- ZELLIJ env var presence

### Resize Handling
On terminal resize:
1. Disable scroll region
2. Recalculate dimensions
3. Re-enable if still appropriate

## Tests Required
- [ ] Scroll region setup/teardown
- [ ] Logs scroll within region
- [ ] UI stays pinned at bottom
- [ ] Mux detection works
- [ ] Resize re-anchoring
- [ ] PTY test: scroll region behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] DECSTBM used correctly
- [ ] Capability gating works
- [ ] Mux passthrough disabled
- [ ] Fallback to overlay works
- [ ] Clean restore on exit

+{"id":"bd-397","title":"Create adversarial tests for escape injection","description":"TESTING - ADVERSARIAL SECURITY TESTS

## Background
FrankenTUI must be secure against untrusted content. Escape sequences in user-provided text could corrupt the terminal or execute malicious sequences. This implements ADR-006 (Sanitize-by-Default).

## Threat Model

### Attack Vectors
1. **Log injection**: Untrusted output contains escape sequences
2. **Title injection**: OSC sequences to change window title
3. **Clipboard injection**: OSC 52 to set clipboard
4. **Cursor corruption**: CSI sequences to move cursor
5. **Mode hijacking**: Enabling raw mode, mouse, etc.

### Example Malicious Inputs
- "\x1b[2J" - Clear screen
- "\x1b[H" - Home cursor
- "\x1b]0;pwned\x07" - Set window title
- "\x1b]52;c;SGVsbG8=\x07" - Set clipboard
- "\x1b[?1049h" - Enter alt screen
- "\x1b[?1000h" - Enable mouse
- "\x1b[31mred\x1b[0m" - Color injection

## Technical Requirements

### Sanitizer Implementation
pub fn sanitize_for_display(input: &str) -> Cow<str> {
    // Fast path: no control characters
    if !input.bytes().any(|b| b < 0x20 || b == 0x7F || b == 0x1B) {
        return Cow::Borrowed(input);
    }
    
    // Slow path: filter control sequences
    let mut output = String::with_capacity(input.len());
    let mut chars = input.chars().peekable();
    
    while let Some(c) = chars.next() {
        match c {
            '\x1b' => skip_escape_sequence(&mut chars),
            c if c.is_control() && c != '\n' && c != '\t' => {
                // Replace with visual representation
                output.push_str(&format!("^{}", (c as u8 + 64) as char));
            }
            _ => output.push(c),
        }
    }
    
    Cow::Owned(output)
}

### Test Categories

#### 1. Escape Sequence Filtering
- [ ] ESC [ ... (CSI) filtered
- [ ] ESC ] ... (OSC) filtered
- [ ] ESC P ... (DCS) filtered
- [ ] ESC _ ... (APC) filtered
- [ ] ESC ^ ... (PM) filtered

#### 2. Control Character Handling
- [ ] NUL (0x00) filtered
- [ ] BEL (0x07) filtered
- [ ] BS (0x08) filtered
- [ ] Tab (0x09) preserved
- [ ] LF (0x0A) preserved
- [ ] CR (0x0D) handled
- [ ] DEL (0x7F) filtered

#### 3. Incomplete Sequence Handling
- [ ] Truncated ESC at end
- [ ] Truncated CSI at end
- [ ] Truncated OSC at end
- [ ] Invalid sequence bytes

#### 4. Raw Passthrough (Explicit Opt-In)
- [ ] raw_text() API exists
- [ ] Loud documentation warning
- [ ] Works for ANSI colors when intended

## PTY Tests

### Test Script
for payload in "${MALICIOUS_PAYLOADS[@]}"; do
    # Run payload through ftui log viewer
    echo "$payload" | ftui-test-harness
    
    # Verify terminal state unchanged
    verify_terminal_state
    
    # Verify no escape sequences in output
    verify_no_escapes
done

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All ESC/CSI/OSC/DCS/APC filtered by default
- [ ] Control characters filtered except tab/newline
- [ ] Fast path for clean input (no allocation)
- [ ] Raw passthrough API documented with warnings

+{"id":"bd-3e8","title":"Implement structured logging with tracing","description":"INFRASTRUCTURE - LOGGING & TRACING

## Background
Comprehensive logging is critical for debugging terminal rendering issues, performance analysis, and understanding execution flow. Must support both development debugging and production diagnostics.

## Technical Requirements

### Tracing Infrastructure
- Use tracing crate for structured, async-aware logging
- Log levels: ERROR, WARN, INFO, DEBUG, TRACE
- Span-based instrumentation for render pipeline
- Subscriber configuration for:
  - Development: pretty-printed to stderr
  - Testing: captured for assertions
  - Production: JSON structured output (optional)

### Key Instrumentation Points
- Frame lifecycle: start → render → diff → present → complete
- Widget rendering: widget type, area, render time
- Buffer operations: resize, clear, set (at TRACE level)
- Input parsing: raw bytes → parsed events
- State changes: model updates, command execution
- Error conditions: with full context

### Log Format
Development:
  2026-01-31T12:00:00.123Z INFO ftui::render frame_start frame=42 widgets=15
  2026-01-31T12:00:00.125Z DEBUG ftui::diff changed_cells=120 skip_rows=10
  
Testing (structured capture):
  LogEntry { level: INFO, target: "ftui::render", msg: "frame_start", ... }

### Feature Gates
- feature = "tracing" - enables all instrumentation
- feature = "tracing-json" - JSON subscriber for production
- Default: tracing disabled for minimal overhead

## Design Rationale
Terminal UIs are notoriously hard to debug. Structured tracing with spans enables understanding complex render pipelines. Feature-gated to avoid overhead in release builds.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] tracing subscriber configuration
- [ ] Frame-level span instrumentation
- [ ] Widget render spans with timing
- [ ] Input parsing tracing
- [ ] Error context capture
- [ ] Test log capture mechanism
- [ ] Feature gate documentation
- [ ] Example showing log output

## Performance Budget
- Disabled: zero overhead (compiled out)
- Enabled: < 1µs per span enter/exit

+{"id":"bd-fbp","title":"Implement inline mode overlay redraw baseline","description":"PHASE 2.5 - INLINE MODE IMPLEMENTATION (CRITICAL)

## Background
Inline mode is a NON-NEGOTIABLE requirement. The overlay redraw baseline is the correctness foundation that works everywhere, before optimization.

## Technical Requirements (from Plan Section D)

### Overlay Redraw Strategy
1. Save cursor position (DECSC or manual tracking)
2. Move cursor to UI region start
3. Clear UI region only (never full screen!)
4. Render UI frame
5. Restore cursor position (DECRC)

### UI Region Anchoring
- Bottom-anchored (default): UI at bottom, logs scroll above
- Top-anchored (optional): UI at top, logs scroll below
- Configurable height: ui_height parameter

### Log Write Coordination
write_log() must:
1. Suspend UI temporarily
2. Print log line to scrollback region
3. Re-render UI in its anchored position
4. Maintain cursor contract

### Cursor Contract
After any present():
- Cursor is at the correct position per active policy
- UI region is intact
- Scrollback is preserved

### Never Clear Full Screen
The DECRC/ED2 pattern that clears full screen is FORBIDDEN in inline mode.
Only clear the bounded UI region using EL (erase line) or localized ED.

## Implementation

pub struct InlinePresenter {
    ui_height: u16,
    anchor: Anchor,  // Bottom or Top
    cursor_saved: bool,
}

impl InlinePresenter {
    pub fn present(&mut self, buffer: &Buffer, writer: &mut TerminalWriter) {
        // 1. Save cursor
        writer.write_all(b"\x1b7")?;  // DECSC
        
        // 2. Move to UI region
        let (_, term_height) = writer.size();
        let ui_start = match self.anchor {
            Anchor::Bottom => term_height - self.ui_height,
            Anchor::Top => 0,
        };
        writer.move_to(0, ui_start)?;
        
        // 3. Clear UI region only (line by line)
        for row in 0..self.ui_height {
            writer.write_all(b"\x1b[2K")?;  // EL - clear line
            if row < self.ui_height - 1 {
                writer.write_all(b"\n")?;
            }
        }
        
        // 4. Move back and render
        writer.move_to(0, ui_start)?;
        // ... emit diff ...
        
        // 5. Restore cursor
        writer.write_all(b"\x1b8")?;  // DECRC
    }
}

## Tests Required
- [ ] UI renders at correct position (bottom/top)
- [ ] Scrollback is preserved after present()
- [ ] Cursor returns to correct position
- [ ] Log writes don't corrupt UI
- [ ] Resize re-anchors correctly
- [ ] PTY test: verify no scrollback corruption

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Overlay redraw works on all terminals
- [ ] Cursor contract maintained
- [ ] UI region bounded, never full-screen clear
- [ ] Log coordination implemented
- [ ] PTY tests pass

## Performance Budget
- Overlay redraw: < 2ms (acceptable for correctness baseline)

 {"id":"bd-yzu","title":"Implement Flex layout with constraint solving","description":"PHASE 5 - FLEX LAYOUT

## Background
Flex layout provides 1D constraint-based sizing for rows/columns. Core layout primitive that most complex layouts build upon. Based on CSS Flexbox concepts adapted for terminal grid.

## Technical Requirements
- Flex struct with direction (Row/Column)
- Constraint types:
  - Fixed(u16): exact size
  - Percent(f32): percentage of parent
  - Min(u16): minimum size, grow to fill
  - Max(u16): maximum size, shrink to fit
  - Ratio(u16, u16): proportional sharing
- Constraint solving algorithm:
  1. Allocate Fixed constraints first
  2. Calculate Percent based on remaining space
  3. Distribute remainder to Min/Ratio proportionally
  4. Clamp Max constraints
- Gap/spacing between items
- Cross-axis alignment: Start, Center, End, Stretch

## Design Rationale
Flex is simpler than full 2D grid but handles 90% of TUI layouts. Constraint types mirror Ratatui's proven model. Solving algorithm is deterministic for predictable layouts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Flex struct with direction and items
- [ ] Full constraint type enum
- [ ] Deterministic constraint solver
- [ ] Gap/margin support
- [ ] Cross-axis alignment
- [ ] Nested Flex support
- [ ] Property tests for constraint invariants
- [ ] Edge case handling (overflow, zero space)

## Performance Budget
- Layout solve for 20 items: < 100µs
- No allocations for typical layouts (stack buffer)
